name: U.S. History
description: A survey of United States history from colonization through the modern era — covering founding ideals, expansion, Civil War, industrialization, the World Wars, the Cold War, and contemporary America.

modules.title: Colonial America & the Road to Revolution
modules.titleZh: 殖民地美洲与革命之路
modules.objectives: Describe the settlement of the thirteen colonies; analyze colonial life and the causes of the American Revolution. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Colonial America — for a visual explanation of Colonial America & the Road to Revolution before guided practice
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Colonial America reading & questions
modules.assignment: Compare the economies and social structures of the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies. Submit a three-column comparison chart using at least two pieces of evidence per region, then write a 300-word explanation of which regional differences later contributed to conflicts in American history and why. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Colonial America & the Road to Revolution module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: The American Revolution & Founding Documents
modules.objectives: Explain the causes and key events of the Revolution; analyze the Declaration of Independence and Articles of Confederation. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — The American Revolution 1763–1783
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — American Revolution article & questions
modules.assignment: Analyze the Declaration of Independence. Identify three grievances listed by Jefferson and explain how each connects to Enlightenment ideas about natural rights. Submit an annotated excerpt with the three grievances highlighted, a short explanation for each connection, and a concluding paragraph on how the document justified revolution. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the The American Revolution & Founding Documents module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: The Constitution & Bill of Rights
modules.objectives: Describe the Constitutional Convention; explain the three branches of government, checks and balances, and the Bill of Rights. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Creating a New Government 1776–1790s
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Constitution article & questions
modules.assignment: Explain how the system of checks and balances prevents any one branch of government from becoming too powerful. Submit a diagram of the three branches with at least six checks labeled, give one historical or contemporary example for each branch checking another, and write a 250-word explanation of why separation of powers matters. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the The Constitution & Bill of Rights module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Westward Expansion & Manifest Destiny
modules.objectives: Trace U.S. expansion from the Louisiana Purchase to the Pacific; analyze the impact on Native Americans and Mexicans. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Westward Expansion and Reform 1815–1880
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Manifest Destiny article & questions
modules.assignment: Write a 300-word essay from two perspectives — a white settler and a Cherokee person — on the forced removal known as the Trail of Tears. Use at least two source-based details for each perspective, explain what Manifest Destiny meant to each group, and add a final reflection distinguishing historical empathy from endorsing harmful actions. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Westward Expansion & Manifest Destiny module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Slavery & the Road to Civil War
modules.objectives: Explain how slavery expanded and became politically contested; analyze key events leading to Southern secession. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Civil War and Reconstruction
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Causes of the Civil War
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Causes of the Civil War article & questions
modules.assignment: Compare the Missouri Compromise (1820), Compromise of 1850, and Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854). Submit a timeline and comparison chart explaining what each law attempted, how it addressed the slavery crisis, which groups opposed it, and why each compromise ultimately failed to prevent conflict. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Slavery & the Road to Civil War module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: The Civil War
modules.objectives: Identify the causes, major turning-point battles, and consequences of the Civil War; analyze the Emancipation Proclamation. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — A House Divided: The Civil War
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The Civil War — for a visual explanation of The Civil War before guided practice
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Civil War overview & questions
modules.assignment: Analyze the Emancipation Proclamation. Submit a paragraph explaining what it actually freed and what it did not free, then write three evidence-backed sections on its military, political, and moral significance. Include one quoted phrase or paraphrased detail from the document with citation information. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the The Civil War module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Reconstruction & the Gilded Age
modules.objectives: Explain Reconstruction policies and their failure; describe industrialization and the rise of big business. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.assignment: Explain how the Compromise of 1877 effectively ended Reconstruction. Submit a cause-and-effect chart showing the election dispute, the political bargain, withdrawal of federal troops, and the rise of Jim Crow. Then write a 300-word explanation of the long-term consequences for African Americans in the South, using at least two concrete examples. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Reconstruction & the Gilded Age module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Immigration, Urbanization & Progressivism
modules.titleZh: 移民、城市化与进步主义
modules.objectives: Describe the waves of immigration and urban growth; analyze Progressive Era reforms and the women's suffrage movement. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Progressive Era to New Era 1900–1929
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The Progressive Era — for a visual explanation of Immigration, Urbanization & Progressivism before guided practice
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Progressive Era article & questions
modules.assignment: Choose one muckraker (e.g., Upton Sinclair, Ida Tarbell, Jacob Riis). Describe what they exposed and what reform their work helped produce. Submit your work as a document, image, or link showing the steps, evidence, and final response. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Immigration, Urbanization & Progressivism module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

questions.question: The Mayflower Compact (1620) is significant because it:
options: Declared independence from Britain
Established the first written self-government agreement in the colonies
Created the House of Burgesses
Granted religious freedom to all colonists
questions.answer: Established the first written self-government agreement in the colonies
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Established the first written self-government agreement in the colonies.

questions.question: The phrase "no taxation without representation" referred to colonists' objection to:
options: High import tariffs on luxury goods
British laws taxing the colonies without colonial representation in Parliament
Paying for the French and Indian War
British troops living in colonial homes
questions.answer: British laws taxing the colonies without colonial representation in Parliament
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: British laws taxing the colonies without colonial representation in Parliament.

questions.question: The opening line of the Declaration of Independence — "We hold these truths to be self-evident" — draws most directly from:
options: The Magna Carta
The English Bill of Rights
Enlightenment philosophy, particularly John Locke
Puritan theology
questions.answer: Enlightenment philosophy, particularly John Locke
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Enlightenment philosophy, particularly John Locke. This reinforces the U.S. History midterm concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: Why did the Articles of Confederation create serious problems for the national government?
options: They gave Congress no power to tax or regulate commerce.
They created a powerful national court system.
They allowed Congress to appoint a king.
They ended state governments entirely.
questions.answer: They gave Congress no power to tax or regulate commerce.
questions.explanation: Under the Articles of Confederation, Congress lacked key powers such as taxation and regulation of interstate commerce, making the national government weak.

questions.question: Which Constitutional principle divides power between the national and state governments?
options: Separation of powers
Federalism
Judicial review
Popular sovereignty
questions.answer: Federalism
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Federalism. The explanation asks students to connect the answer with cause, evidence, or consequence rather than memorizing a term.

questions.question: The Louisiana Purchase (1803) roughly doubled U.S. territory and was made from:
options: Spain
Britain
France (Napoleon)
Mexico
questions.answer: France (Napoleon)
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: France (Napoleon). The explanation asks students to connect the answer with cause, evidence, or consequence rather than memorizing a term.

questions.question: The Trail of Tears (1838–39) refers to:
options: The migration of settlers along the Oregon Trail
The forced relocation of the Cherokee Nation from Georgia to Oklahoma
The retreat of the U.S. Army after the Battle of Little Bighorn
Wagon trains crossing the Sierra Nevada mountains
questions.answer: The forced relocation of the Cherokee Nation from Georgia to Oklahoma
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: The forced relocation of the Cherokee Nation from Georgia to Oklahoma.

questions.question: How did the Missouri Compromise of 1820 attempt to address the slavery debate?
options: It abolished slavery in all new territories.
It admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state while setting a latitude line for slavery in much of the Louisiana Purchase.
It allowed only the president to decide whether slavery could expand.
It required all states to hold immediate votes on abolition.
questions.answer: It admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state while setting a latitude line for slavery in much of the Louisiana Purchase.
questions.explanation: The compromise admitted Missouri as a slave state, Maine as a free state, and generally restricted slavery north of 36 degrees 30 minutes in the Louisiana Purchase, except Missouri.

questions.question: The Emancipation Proclamation (1863) declared freedom for enslaved people:
options: In all U.S. states immediately
Only in Confederate states still in rebellion
In border states loyal to the Union
Only after the war ended
questions.answer: Only in Confederate states still in rebellion
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Only in Confederate states still in rebellion. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from U.S. History midterm concepts.

questions.question: Which battle is considered the turning point of the Civil War, halting the Confederacy's northern advance?
options: Bull Run
Antietam
Gettysburg
Vicksburg
questions.answer: Gettysburg
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Gettysburg. The explanation asks students to connect the answer with cause, evidence, or consequence rather than memorizing a term.

questions.question: The 13th Amendment to the Constitution (1865) abolished:
options: Racial segregation
Slavery throughout the United States
Poll taxes
Literacy tests for voting
questions.answer: Slavery throughout the United States
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Slavery throughout the United States. This reinforces the U.S. History midterm concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: The term "Robber Barons" is commonly used to describe:
options: Small farmers who opposed railroad expansion
Powerful industrial capitalists who dominated or monopolized industries
Union organizers who led factory strikes
Progressive reformers who supported antitrust laws
questions.answer: Powerful industrial capitalists who dominated or monopolized industries
questions.explanation: The label "Robber Barons" criticized powerful late-19th-century industrialists accused of monopolistic practices and harsh labor policies.

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'popular sovereignty' and describe how it contributed to the violence known as 'Bleeding Kansas' in the 1850s.
questions.answer: Popular sovereignty meant that settlers in a new territory would vote to decide whether slavery would be permitted. The Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) applied this principle to Kansas, drawing pro-slavery and anti-slavery settlers into the territory to influence the vote. The resulting competition led to ballot fraud, attacks, and guerrilla warfare between the two factions, earning the territory the name 'Bleeding Kansas' and demonstrating that popular sovereignty could not peacefully resolve the slavery question.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Popular sovereignty meant that settlers in a new territory would vote to decide whether slavery would be permitted. The Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) applied this principle to Kansas, drawing pro-slavery and anti-slavery settlers into the territory to influence the vote. The resulting competition led to ballot fraud, attacks, and guerrilla warfare between the two factions, earning the territory the name 'Bleeding Kansas' and demonstrating that popular sovereignty could not peacefully resolve the slavery question..

questions.question: The Homestead Act of 1862 encouraged western settlement by:
options: Paying settlers a cash bounty for moving west
Offering 160 acres of public land to settlers who would farm it for five years
Providing free railroad tickets to the frontier
Allowing purchase of Native American lands at low prices
questions.answer: Offering 160 acres of public land to settlers who would farm it for five years
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Offering 160 acres of public land to settlers who would farm it for five years.

questions.question: What were the three Reconstruction Amendments (13th, 14th, 15th), and why did Reconstruction ultimately fail to deliver lasting equality for African Americans?
questions.answer: The 13th Amendment abolished slavery, the 14th Amendment guaranteed equal citizenship and due process, and the 15th Amendment prohibited denying the right to vote based on race. Reconstruction failed because of the Compromise of 1877, which withdrew federal troops from the South; the rise of the Ku Klux Klan and white supremacist violence; the emergence of Black Codes and later Jim Crow laws; sharecropping systems that kept freedpeople in economic servitude; and the Supreme Court's narrow interpretation of the 14th Amendment that permitted segregation.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: The 13th Amendment abolished slavery, the 14th Amendment guaranteed equal citizenship and due process, and the 15th Amendment prohibited denying the right to vote based on race. Reconstruction failed because of the Compromise of 1877, which withdrew federal troops from the South; the rise of the Ku Klux Klan and white supremacist violence; the emergence of Black Codes and later Jim Crow laws; sharecropping systems that kept freedpeople in economic servitude; and the Supreme Court's narrow interpretation of the 14th Amendment that permitted segregation..

questions.question: The Boston Tea Party of 1773 was mainly a protest against:
options: A tax on all colonial exports
The Tea Act and the British East India Company's favored position in colonial tea sales
The Quartering Act's housing requirements
The closing of the port of New York
questions.answer: The Tea Act and the British East India Company's favored position in colonial tea sales
questions.explanation: Colonists protested the Tea Act, which gave the British East India Company an advantage in colonial tea sales and symbolized taxation without colonial representation.

questions.question: The Great Compromise at the Constitutional Convention created:
options: The Electoral College and popular vote
A bicameral legislature with a Senate (equal state representation) and House (proportional representation)
The Three-Fifths Compromise for enslaved people
A strong executive with veto power
questions.answer: A bicameral legislature with a Senate (equal state representation) and House (proportional representation)
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: A bicameral legislature with a Senate (equal state representation) and House (proportional representation).

questions.question: Manifest Destiny expressed the belief that:
options: America should remain isolated from European affairs
The United States was divinely destined to expand across the North American continent
Native Americans should be assimilated into U.S. culture
The federal government should control all western lands
questions.answer: The United States was divinely destined to expand across the North American continent
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: The United States was divinely destined to expand across the North American continent.

questions.question: The Dred Scott v. Sandford decision (1857) ruled that:
options: Slavery was unconstitutional in northern states
African Americans were not citizens and had no right to sue in federal courts
Congress could ban slavery in territories
Enslaved people who reached free states were automatically free
questions.answer: African Americans were not citizens and had no right to sue in federal courts
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: African Americans were not citizens and had no right to sue in federal courts.

questions.question: Which battle is often considered the turning point of the Civil War in the East?
options: Gettysburg, Pennsylvania
Fort Sumter, South Carolina
Bull Run, Virginia
Appomattox Court House, Virginia
questions.answer: Gettysburg, Pennsylvania
questions.explanation: The Union victory at Gettysburg in July 1863 stopped Lee's invasion of the North and is often treated as a major turning point in the eastern theater.

questions.question: Muckrakers were Progressive Era journalists who:
options: Supported big business and opposed government regulation
Investigated and exposed corruption, unsafe conditions, and social injustices
Covered foreign policy and diplomacy
Wrote propaganda supporting U.S. entry into WWI
questions.answer: Investigated and exposed corruption, unsafe conditions, and social injustices
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Investigated and exposed corruption, unsafe conditions, and social injustices.

questions.question: The Zimmermann Telegram (1917) helped push the U.S. into WWI by:
options: Promising Germany's alliance with Mexico against the United States
Revealing Germany's plan to sink American ships
Offering peace terms to the Allied powers
Declaring war on the United States
questions.answer: Promising Germany's alliance with Mexico against the United States
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Promising Germany's alliance with Mexico against the United States.

questions.question: The stock market crash of October 1929 contributed to the Great Depression by:
options: Causing a natural drought that destroyed crops
Wiping out investors' savings and triggering bank failures and credit contraction
Leading to a global trade war that eliminated American exports overnight
Causing the federal government to raise taxes sharply
questions.answer: Wiping out investors' savings and triggering bank failures and credit contraction
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Wiping out investors' savings and triggering bank failures and credit contraction.

questions.question: What was a direct result of the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941?
options: The United States entered World War II after declaring war on Japan.
The United States immediately withdrew from the Pacific.
The League of Nations declared war on Japan.
The United States remained neutral in World War II.
questions.answer: The United States entered World War II after declaring war on Japan.
questions.explanation: After Pearl Harbor, the United States declared war on Japan and formally entered World War II.

questions.question: The policy of containment, as articulated by George Kennan and the Truman Doctrine, aimed to:
options: Eliminate communism worldwide through military force
Prevent the further spread of Soviet communist influence beyond its existing borders
Negotiate arms reduction with the Soviet Union
Promote democracy in Western Europe only
questions.answer: Prevent the further spread of Soviet communist influence beyond its existing borders
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Prevent the further spread of Soviet communist influence beyond its existing borders.

questions.question: Brown v. Board of Education (1954) overturned the precedent set by:
options: Dred Scott v. Sandford
Marbury v. Madison
Plessy v. Ferguson
McCulloch v. Maryland
questions.answer: Plessy v. Ferguson
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Plessy v. Ferguson. Students need to recognize why Plessy v. Ferguson matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibited discrimination based on:
options: Race only
Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin
Race and religion only
Age and disability
questions.answer: Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin. It ties the answer to the historical, civic, geographic, or economic concept being assessed.

questions.question: Reaganomics, or supply-side economics, was based partly on the idea that:
options: Higher taxes on investment would slow inflation by reducing business activity.
Tax cuts and deregulation would encourage investment, production, and economic growth.
The federal government should nationalize major industries.
The economy should be managed mainly through wage and price controls.
questions.answer: Tax cuts and deregulation would encourage investment, production, and economic growth.
questions.explanation: Supply-side policy emphasized tax cuts, deregulation, and incentives for investment as ways to encourage economic growth.

questions.question: The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 symbolized:
options: The beginning of the Cold War
Germany's defeat in WWII
The collapse of communist governments in Eastern Europe and the end of the Cold War
The reunification of Germany under communist rule
questions.answer: The collapse of communist governments in Eastern Europe and the end of the Cold War
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: The collapse of communist governments in Eastern Europe and the end of the Cold War.

questions.question: The USA PATRIOT Act (2001), passed after 9/11, was controversial because:
options: It declared war on Afghanistan without Congressional approval
Critics argued it expanded government surveillance powers in ways that threatened civil liberties
It authorized the use of nuclear weapons
It created a national ID card system
questions.answer: Critics argued it expanded government surveillance powers in ways that threatened civil liberties
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Critics argued it expanded government surveillance powers in ways that threatened civil liberties.

questions.question: The internment of Japanese Americans during WWII was authorized by:
options: The Espionage Act
Executive Order 9066
The Alien and Sedition Acts
A Supreme Court ruling
questions.answer: Executive Order 9066
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Executive Order 9066. It ties the answer to the historical, civic, geographic, or economic concept being assessed.

questions.question: The Montgomery Bus Boycott of 1955-56 was triggered by:
options: A federal court ruling desegregating public transit
Rosa Parks' arrest after she refused to give up her bus seat to a white passenger
The passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964
The opening of the March on Washington
questions.answer: Rosa Parks' arrest after she refused to give up her bus seat to a white passenger
questions.explanation: The boycott began after Rosa Parks was arrested in Montgomery, Alabama, for refusing to give up her seat under segregated bus rules.

questions.question: Describe the Marshall Plan and explain why it was both a humanitarian and a strategic Cold War decision for the United States.
questions.answer: The Marshall Plan (1948) provided over $13 billion in U.S. economic aid to rebuild war-devastated Western European nations after WWII. Humanitarianly, it fed starving populations and rebuilt destroyed infrastructure. Strategically, the U.S. feared that economic desperation would make Western European nations vulnerable to communist political movements and Soviet influence — as had already occurred in Eastern Europe. By rebuilding strong, prosperous democracies, the U.S. created stable trading partners and reliable Cold War allies, while demonstrating that capitalism and democracy could outperform communism at improving living standards.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: The Marshall Plan (1948) provided over $13 billion in U.S. economic aid to rebuild war-devastated Western European nations after WWII. Humanitarianly, it fed starving populations and rebuilt destroyed infrastructure. Strategically, the U.S. feared that economic desperation would make Western European nations vulnerable to communist political movements and Soviet influence — as had already occurred in Eastern Europe. By rebuilding strong, prosperous democracies, the U.S. created stable trading partners and reliable Cold War allies, while demonstrating that capitalism and democracy could outperform communism at improving living standards..

questions.question: How did the Civil Rights Movement of the 1950s and 1960s employ nonviolent direct action, and what were two major legislative achievements that resulted from this activism?
questions.answer: Civil rights activists used nonviolent direct action tactics inspired by Mahatma Gandhi, including sit-ins (like the Greensboro sit-ins of 1960), freedom rides on interstate buses, marches (like the March on Washington in 1963 and the Selma-to-Montgomery marches of 1965), and economic boycotts. These tactics exposed the brutality of segregation to national and international audiences, building public pressure for change. Two major legislative achievements were the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which banned discrimination in public accommodations and employment, and the Voting Rights Act of 1965, which prohibited discriminatory voting practices like literacy tests that had disenfranchised Black voters across the South.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Civil rights activists used nonviolent direct action tactics inspired by Mahatma Gandhi, including sit-ins (like the Greensboro sit-ins of 1960), freedom rides on interstate buses, marches (like the March on Washington in 1963 and the Selma-to-Montgomery marches of 1965), and economic boycotts. These tactics exposed the brutality of segregation to national and international audiences, building public pressure for change. Two major legislative achievements were the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which banned discrimination in public accommodations and employment, and the Voting Rights Act of 1965, which prohibited discriminatory voting practices like literacy tests that had disenfranchised Black voters across the South..

questions.question: How did globalization from the 1990s onward reshape the American economy, and what political tensions did these changes produce?
questions.answer: Globalization accelerated through trade agreements like NAFTA (1994) and the WTO, the rise of the internet, and containerized shipping. It integrated the U.S. economy into global supply chains, lowering consumer prices and expanding export markets. However, it also led to manufacturing job losses as companies moved production to lower-wage countries, contributing to deindustrialization in the Rust Belt. Growing economic inequality between college-educated workers who benefited and working-class communities that lost factory jobs produced significant political tension — fueling trade protectionist sentiment, debates about immigration, and the political polarization that characterized early 21st-century American politics.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Globalization accelerated through trade agreements like NAFTA (1994) and the WTO, the rise of the internet, and containerized shipping. It integrated the U.S. economy into global supply chains, lowering consumer prices and expanding export markets. However, it also led to manufacturing job losses as companies moved production to lower-wage countries, contributing to deindustrialization in the Rust Belt. Growing economic inequality between college-educated workers who benefited and working-class communities that lost factory jobs produced significant political tension — fueling trade protectionist sentiment, debates about immigration, and the political polarization that characterized early 21st-century American politics..

quizQuestions.question: The Jamestown settlement (1607) was founded primarily for:
options: Religious freedom
Economic profit by the Virginia Company
Escaping political persecution
Military strategic purposes
quizQuestions.answer: Economic profit by the Virginia Company
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Economic profit by the Virginia Company. The explanation asks students to connect the answer with cause, evidence, or consequence rather than memorizing a term.

quizQuestions.question: The Mayflower Compact is historically significant because it:
options: Declared independence from England
Established the first example of self-government by agreement in the colonies
Created the first colonial legislature
Granted land rights to Pilgrim settlers
quizQuestions.answer: Established the first example of self-government by agreement in the colonies
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Established the first example of self-government by agreement in the colonies.

quizQuestions.question: The Jamestown settlement, founded in 1607, was established primarily for:
options: Religious freedom for Puritans
Economic profit by the Virginia Company
A Spanish military alliance
A French fur-trading mission
quizQuestions.answer: Economic profit by the Virginia Company
quizQuestions.explanation: Jamestown was founded by the Virginia Company, a joint-stock company seeking profit.

quizQuestions.question: Thomas Paine's 'Common Sense' (1776) argued that:
options: The colonies should seek reconciliation with Britain
A monarchy was the best form of government
American independence was necessary and a republic was superior to monarchy
The Continental Army needed more funding
quizQuestions.answer: American independence was necessary and a republic was superior to monarchy
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: American independence was necessary and a republic was superior to monarchy.

quizQuestions.question: The Battle of Saratoga (1777) was a turning point in the Revolution because it:
options: Captured the British capital of New York City
Convinced France to formally ally with the Americans
Led directly to the British surrender at Yorktown
Was the first American military victory
quizQuestions.answer: Convinced France to formally ally with the Americans
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Convinced France to formally ally with the Americans.

quizQuestions.question: The Articles of Confederation created a central government that:
options: Could tax citizens directly
Had a powerful executive branch
Relied on states to voluntarily provide funds and soldiers
Controlled interstate commerce
quizQuestions.answer: Relied on states to voluntarily provide funds and soldiers
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Relied on states to voluntarily provide funds and soldiers.

quizQuestions.question: Thomas Paine's Common Sense argued that:
options: The colonies should reconcile permanently with the king
American independence was necessary and republican government was preferable to monarchy
Only Parliament should tax the colonies
The Articles of Confederation should be replaced immediately
quizQuestions.answer: American independence was necessary and republican government was preferable to monarchy
quizQuestions.explanation: Common Sense made a forceful public argument for independence and republican government.

quizQuestions.question: The Constitutional Convention took place in:
options: Boston
New York City
Philadelphia
Washington, D.C.
quizQuestions.answer: Philadelphia
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Philadelphia. The explanation asks students to connect the answer with cause, evidence, or consequence rather than memorizing a term.

quizQuestions.question: The system of checks and balances ensures that:
options: The president appoints all federal judges
Each branch of government can limit the power of the other branches
Congress has final authority over all laws
The Supreme Court can override any election result
quizQuestions.answer: Each branch of government can limit the power of the other branches
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Each branch of government can limit the power of the other branches.

quizQuestions.question: The First Amendment to the Constitution protects:
options: The right to bear arms
Freedom of speech, religion, press, assembly, and petition
Protection against unreasonable searches
The right to a fair trial
quizQuestions.answer: Freedom of speech, religion, press, assembly, and petition
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Freedom of speech, religion, press, assembly, and petition.

quizQuestions.question: President Thomas Jefferson purchased the Louisiana Territory in 1803 from:
options: Britain
Spain
France
Mexico
quizQuestions.answer: France
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: France. It connects the correct answer to Westward Expansion & Manifest Destiny and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: The expedition of Lewis and Clark (1804–06) was sent to:
options: Establish military forts in the West
Explore the new Louisiana Territory and find a route to the Pacific
Negotiate treaties with Native American tribes
Map the Rocky Mountains for future railroads
quizQuestions.answer: Explore the new Louisiana Territory and find a route to the Pacific
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Explore the new Louisiana Territory and find a route to the Pacific.

quizQuestions.question: The Indian Removal Act (1830) was signed by President:
options: Thomas Jefferson
James Monroe
Andrew Jackson
James K. Polk
quizQuestions.answer: Andrew Jackson
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Andrew Jackson. It connects the correct answer to Westward Expansion & Manifest Destiny and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: The Mexican-American War (1846–48) resulted in the U.S. acquiring:
options: Texas only
California, New Mexico, and other territories (the Mexican Cession)
The entire Mexican nation
Cuba and Puerto Rico
quizQuestions.answer: California, New Mexico, and other territories (the Mexican Cession)
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: California, New Mexico, and other territories (the Mexican Cession).

quizQuestions.question: Harriet Beecher Stowe's novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin' (1852) contributed to the Civil War by:
options: Providing military strategy to the Union Army
Strengthening the abolitionist movement by portraying the horrors of slavery
Convincing Southern states to secede
Helping elect Abraham Lincoln as president
quizQuestions.answer: Strengthening the abolitionist movement by portraying the horrors of slavery
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Strengthening the abolitionist movement by portraying the horrors of slavery.

quizQuestions.question: The Dred Scott decision (1857) declared that:
options: Slavery was legal only in the South
Enslaved people were property, not citizens, with no right to sue in court
Congress had power to ban slavery in territories
Free states must return escaped enslaved people
quizQuestions.answer: Enslaved people were property, not citizens, with no right to sue in court
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Enslaved people were property, not citizens, with no right to sue in court.

quizQuestions.question: Harriet Beecher Stowe's Uncle Tom's Cabin contributed to sectional conflict by:
options: Defending the expansion of slavery into western territories
Strengthening abolitionist sentiment by portraying the cruelty of slavery
Calling for immediate secession by northern states
Arguing that Reconstruction had failed
quizQuestions.answer: Strengthening abolitionist sentiment by portraying the cruelty of slavery
quizQuestions.explanation: The novel reached a wide audience and intensified northern opposition to slavery by depicting its human costs.

quizQuestions.question: The Dred Scott decision of 1857 declared that:
options: African Americans were not citizens and could not sue in federal court
Slavery was legal only in the South
Congress had abolished slavery in all territories
The Emancipation Proclamation was constitutional
quizQuestions.answer: African Americans were not citizens and could not sue in federal court
quizQuestions.explanation: The Supreme Court held that Dred Scott could not sue in federal court because African Americans were not considered U.S. citizens under the decision.

quizQuestions.question: The Civil War began in April 1861 when Confederate forces attacked:
options: Washington, D.C.
Fort Sumter in South Carolina
Gettysburg, Pennsylvania
Manassas, Virginia
quizQuestions.answer: Fort Sumter in South Carolina
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Fort Sumter in South Carolina. It connects the correct answer to The Civil War and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: The Emancipation Proclamation took effect on:
options: January 1, 1863
July 4, 1862
April 9, 1865
December 6, 1865
quizQuestions.answer: January 1, 1863
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: January 1, 1863. The explanation asks students to connect the answer with cause, evidence, or consequence rather than memorizing a term.

quizQuestions.question: General Ulysses S. Grant is most associated with:
options: Leading Confederate forces in Virginia
The Union strategy of total war and victory in the western theater
Defending Fort Sumter
The Union blockade of Confederate ports
quizQuestions.answer: The Union strategy of total war and victory in the western theater
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The Union strategy of total war and victory in the western theater.

quizQuestions.question: Which battle gave the Union control of the Mississippi River?
options: Vicksburg in Mississippi
Fort Sumter in South Carolina
Gettysburg in Pennsylvania
Richmond in Virginia
quizQuestions.answer: Vicksburg in Mississippi
quizQuestions.explanation: The Union victory at Vicksburg in 1863 gave the Union control of the Mississippi River and split the Confederacy.

quizQuestions.question: The 14th Amendment (1868) granted:
options: The right to vote to all male citizens
Citizenship and equal protection under the law to all persons born in the U.S.
The abolition of slavery
Women the right to vote
quizQuestions.answer: Citizenship and equal protection under the law to all persons born in the U.S.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Citizenship and equal protection under the law to all persons born in the U.S..

quizQuestions.question: Sharecropping kept many freedpeople in poverty after the Civil War because:
options: It paid lower wages than factory work
Sharecroppers often fell into debt to landowners and could not leave
The federal government taxed sharecroppers at higher rates
It was illegal in most Southern states
quizQuestions.answer: Sharecroppers often fell into debt to landowners and could not leave
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Sharecroppers often fell into debt to landowners and could not leave.

quizQuestions.question: The Fourteenth Amendment granted:
options: Citizenship and equal protection under the law to persons born or naturalized in the United States
The direct election of senators
The abolition of slavery throughout the United States
The right to vote regardless of sex
quizQuestions.answer: Citizenship and equal protection under the law to persons born or naturalized in the United States
quizQuestions.explanation: The Fourteenth Amendment established birthright citizenship and required equal protection and due process under the law.

quizQuestions.question: The largest wave of immigration to the United States (1880–1920) came primarily from:
options: Northern and Western Europe
Southern and Eastern Europe, as well as Asia
Latin America and the Caribbean
Canada and Australia
quizQuestions.answer: Southern and Eastern Europe, as well as Asia
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Southern and Eastern Europe, as well as Asia. It connects the correct answer to Immigration, Urbanization & Progressivism and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Upton Sinclair's novel 'The Jungle' (1906) exposed:
options: Political corruption in city governments
Unsafe and unsanitary conditions in the meatpacking industry
The exploitation of child miners in Appalachia
Racial discrimination in Southern sharecropping
quizQuestions.answer: Unsafe and unsanitary conditions in the meatpacking industry
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Unsafe and unsanitary conditions in the meatpacking industry.

quizQuestions.question: The 19th Amendment (1920) granted:
options: The right to vote to all male citizens regardless of race
Women the right to vote
Prohibition of alcohol
Income tax authorization to Congress
quizQuestions.answer: Women the right to vote
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Women the right to vote. The explanation asks students to connect the answer with cause, evidence, or consequence rather than memorizing a term.

quizQuestions.question: Compared with earlier immigration waves, many immigrants arriving in the United States from 1880 to 1920 came from:
options: Southern and Eastern Europe, with some immigration from Asia
Only England and Scotland
Only Latin America
Australia and New Zealand
quizQuestions.answer: Southern and Eastern Europe, with some immigration from Asia
quizQuestions.explanation: Late-19th- and early-20th-century immigration included large numbers from Southern and Eastern Europe, along with significant immigration from parts of Asia.
